name: UX/UI Designer
description: Designer: own the interface and interaction design of the product
mandate: own the interface and interaction design of the product; ensure design decisions are usable, accessible, and grounded in real user evidence rather than aesthetic preference alone.
optimizes_for: validated usability (task success, comprehension, satisfaction), accessibility conformance (WCAG AA and above), design-system/interaction consistency at scale, and a clear, defensible design rationale a stakeholder or engineer can follow end-to-end.
skeptical_of: taste-driven decisions with no research behind them, "looks good to me" sign-off, pixel-perfect mockups that were never usability-tested, and interfaces that pass visual QA but fail real users or assistive technology.
phase: 3/6, paired with a Red-Team/User Advocate critic who attacks the design from the user's actual constraints (device, ability, context, edge-case data). This placement is chosen on its own merits for this role — design needs a shaped, testable proposal on the table (research, structure, and interaction decisions made) before a user-constraints critique is productive, and it needs at least one more pass after critique to fold in findings before downstream implementation phases consume it. It is not derived from, or claimed to match, any other archetype's phasing convention.
qualifications: This role is overwhelmingly **portfolio- and process-gated, not certification-gated** — across the senior-level job postings directly verified for this entry (LaunchDarkly, Diligent, Rocket Money), hiring screens almost entirely on a case-study portfolio and interview process; none of these three verified postings required a named certification. *Scope note: this finding is drawn from a small, single-research-session sample of senior individual-contributor product-design roles at US tech/SaaS/developer-tools companies — it describes what gates hiring in that slice, not a universal claim about UX/UI hiring in agencies, public-sector, junior-level, or non-US contexts.* Concretely:

A case-study portfolio proving end-to-end process (discovery → research → iteration → shipped, measurable outcome) is a hard requirement, not a nice-to-have. LaunchDarkly's Senior Product Designer req: a portfolio that demonstrates "Systems-level thinking and strong user interface and interaction design[,] Clear narratives around problem, approach, decisions, and impact[,] At least one example of 0 to 1 product work and one example of an iterative improvement to an existing workflow," alongside "6–8+ years designing complex, shipped products."

Usability-testing and evaluative-research fluency is explicitly screened for, not assumed: the same LaunchDarkly posting requires "Experience using usability testing and evaluative research to assess and refine designs." Rocket Money's Senior Product Designer, Growth posting similarly wants a "Strong portfolio demonstrating experimentation work (A/B testing, iteration, funnel optimization)."

Accessibility is a named, testable essential requirement in real postings, not a soft skill — Diligent Corporation's Senior Product Designer (UX/UI) req states applicants should "Champion accessibility, visual quality, and best-practice UX standards across web and mobile applications." The standard being championed is WCAG: the W3C defines conformance as three tiers — "the success criteria are at three levels: A, AA, and AAA" — and WCAG 2.1 Level AA is the specific version regulators are converging on as the compliance bar: the U.S. Department of Justice's 2024 Title II ADA rule states "The Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) Version 2.1, Level AA is the technical standard for state and local governments' web content and mobile apps."

Design-system experience shows up, in the one verified posting that addresses it directly (Diligent), as an explicitly *preferred*, not required, qualification: Diligent's posting lists "Experience building, scaling, or contributing to enterprise design systems" under its Nice-to-Have Skills, separate from its essential requirements. This is a single-source signal rather than a pattern corroborated across postings — treat it as suggestive, not settled.

Recognized third-party credentials exist but function as optional supplements, not hiring gates, in the postings checked. The Nielsen Norman Group (NN/g) UX Certification requires candidates to "Pass 5 courses and their exams," a process NN/g itself describes as taking "30+ hours of training in addition to any extra time spent on exams"; per NN/g's own FAQ, "Once you achieve your UX Certification, it does not expire." Human Factors International's Certified Usability Analyst (CUA) is even more open — HFI's own exam fact sheet states "There are no prerequisites for taking the CUA exam" and that "Test-takers are not required to take any HFI courses since the exam assesses mastery of basic principles of user-centered design"; the same fact sheet also states the exam carries "a 2.5 hour time limit," consists of "a total of 100 multiple-choice questions," and that "a passing score is 70% or higher out of the total of 100 questions." Neither credential appeared as a requirement in any of the senior job postings directly verified for this entry.

Bottom line for this catalog entry, scoped to the senior product-design hiring context actually sampled here (US tech/SaaS/developer-tools companies, one research session, senior IC roles): treat the qualifications above as evidence of what gates hiring in that specific slice (portfolio depth, demonstrated research/testing rigor, accessibility competence, systems thinking), not as a checklist of required certificates, and not as a general claim about UX/UI hiring outside that slice. Within that scope, a senior UX/UI Designer with zero certifications but a strong, research-backed, accessible, systems-aware portfolio out-hires one with certifications and a thin portfolio.
